drop.rationale: Morphological variant of 'criminal law'/'criminality'; verb form adds no independent doctrinal content and has zero e-Legislation attestation.
Nominalisation of the same verb; redundant with 'criminal law' as the anchor concept and zero attestation.
Abstract noun derived from the same stem; duplicates the conceptual space already covered by 'criminal law' with zero attestation.
Adverbial derivation lacking independent doctrinal identity; redundant near-synonym of 'criminal law'.
Adjectival derivation of 'probation'; no distinct legal concept beyond the canonical noun.
Person-noun derived from 'probation'; collapses into the same concept and adds morphological noise rather than doctrinal variation.
Rare abstract derivation of 'probation' with marginal HK usage and no doctrinal autonomy.
Adjectival derivation of 'extortion'; the offence concept is already captured canonically.
Negation of 'prosecution' rather than an autonomous concept; low attestation and redundant with the canonical form.
Negation of 'parole' with zero e-Legislation attestation; not a freestanding doctrinal category in HK criminal law.
Adjectival/temporal modifier of 'sentencing' with zero attestation; not an independent concept.
Person-noun derived from 'recidivism'; morphological duplicate without distinct doctrinal weight.
Hedged adjectival derivation of 'penal'; zero attestation and no autonomous doctrinal identity.
Sub-doctrinal variant superseded in English law (R v G, 2003) and absent from HK statute; canonical 'recklessness' already covers the general principle.
Paired sub-doctrinal variant of recklessness; subsumed under canonical 'recklessness' and zero e-Legislation attestation.
HK administrative reporting instrument rather than a substantive criminal-law concept; peripheral conceptual centrality.

curation_principles: The curation preserves conceptual density across the substantive architecture of criminal law while pruning morphological derivations that inflate the lexicon without adding doctrinal content. Priority was given to near-synonym consolidation: verbal, adjectival, adverbial and person-noun derivations of a shared stem ('criminalise', 'criminalisation', 'criminality', 'criminally'; 'probationary', 'probationer', 'probationership'; 'extortionate'; 'non-prosecution'; 'non-parole'; 'pre-sentencing'; 'recidivist'; 'quasi-penal') were removed in favour of their canonical nominal anchors, since such derivations introduce surface variation unmatched by any independent legal concept and would conflate form-level noise with genuine semantic structure in embedding space. Two sub-doctrinal labels of recklessness (Caldwell and Cunningham) were also removed: both are jurisprudential refinements subsumed under the canonical 'recklessness' and, crucially, carry zero attestation in HK e-Legislation, reflecting the post-R v G (2003) consolidation. A single administrative HK instrument ('Minor Offences Report') was dropped as peripheral to substantive criminal doctrine. The retained fifty terms cover the full doctrinal spectrum a HK criminal-law treatise would address: substantive offences against the person (homicide, murder, manslaughter, assault, battery), property offences (theft, larceny, robbery, burglary, blackmail, extortion, forgery), offences against public morals and the administration of justice (perjury, subornation of perjury, conspiracy, conspiracy to defraud, corruption, bribery); general principles (actus reus, mens rea, recklessness, inchoate liability, accessory, accomplice); defences and procedural-liability anchors (approbation, reprobation); institutional actors (DPP, ICAC); and sentencing and custodial concepts (sentencing, penalty, penal, imprisonment, incarceration, parole, probation, remand, recidivism). This balance supports the measurement design by providing a doctrinally saturated yet morphologically clean probe of the criminal-law region of meaning-space.
